mission.title: Install and Configure Hami on Kubernetes
mission.description: HAMi is a Heterogeneous AI Computing Virtualization Middleware that allows for efficient management of diverse computing resources like GPUs and NPUs in Kubernetes. Installing HAMi enables better scheduling decisions and resource sharing among pods, enhancing the performance of AI workloads.

steps.title: Add the Helm repository
steps.description:
Add the official Helm chart repository for HAMi to your local Helm client. This allows you to install the HAMi chart directly from the repository.
```bash
helm repo add hami https://koordinator-sh.github.io/charts
helm repo update
```

steps.title: Install the HAMi chart
steps.description:
Install the HAMi chart in a new namespace called 'hami'. This command deploys the necessary resources defined in the chart.
```bash
helm install hami hami/hami-daemon --namespace hami --create-namespace --version 0.1.0
```

steps.title: Verify the installation
steps.description:
Check that the HAMi pods are running successfully. This command lists the pods in the 'hami' namespace.
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace hami
```

steps.title: Set up RBAC for HAMi
steps.description:
Create a Role and RoleBinding to grant the necessary permissions for HAMi to operate correctly in the cluster.
```bash
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: Role
metadata:
  namespace: hami
  name: hami-role
rules:
- apiGroups: ["*"]
  resources: ["pods", "pods/status", "pods/exec"]
  verbs: ["get", "list", "watch", "create", "delete", "update"]
EOF

kubectl create rolebinding hami-role-binding --role=hami-role --serviceaccount=hami:hami --namespace=hami
```

uninstall.title: Remove the Helm release
uninstall.description:
Uninstall the HAMi release from the cluster. This command removes all resources associated with the release.
```bash
helm uninstall hami --namespace hami
```

uninstall.title: Clean up CRDs and resources
uninstall.description:
Delete any Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs) that were created during the installation. This ensures no leftover resources remain.
```bash
kubectl delete crd hamiresources.hami.io
```

uninstall.title: Remove the namespace
uninstall.description:
Delete the 'hami' namespace to clean up any remaining resources. This command will remove all resources in that namespace.
```bash
kubectl delete namespace hami
```

upgrade.title: Backup current state
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, it's a good practice to back up the current state of your deployment. You can export the current configuration to a YAML file.
```bash
kubectl get all --namespace hami -o yaml > hami-backup.yaml
```

upgrade.title: Update the Helm repository
upgrade.description:
Ensure that your Helm repository is up to date to fetch the latest version of the HAMi chart.
```bash
helm repo update
```

upgrade.title: Upgrade the HAMi release
upgrade.description:
Upgrade the HAMi release to the latest version. Replace '0.1.0' with the new version if available.
```bash
helm upgrade hami hami/hami-daemon --namespace hami --version 0.1.0
```

upgrade.title: Verify the upgrade
upgrade.description:
Check that the HAMi pods are running the new version successfully. This command lists the pods in the 'hami' namespace.
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace hami
```

troubleshooting.title: Pods stuck in Pending state
troubleshooting.description:
If your HAMi pods are stuck in a Pending state, check for resource availability. Use the following command to diagnose:
```bash
kubectl describe pod <pod-name> --namespace hami
```
Ensure that there are enough resources (CPU, memory) available in your cluster.

troubleshooting.title: Pods crash with error
troubleshooting.description:
If your HAMi pods are crashing, check the logs for error messages. Use the following command:
```bash
kubectl logs <pod-name> --namespace hami
```
This will help identify any issues with the application or configuration.

troubleshooting.title: RBAC permission errors
troubleshooting.description:
If you encounter permission errors, verify that the Role and RoleBinding were created correctly. Check the bindings with:
```bash
kubectl get rolebinding --namespace hami
```
Ensure that the service account used by HAMi has the necessary permissions.

resolution.summary: A successful installation of HAMi will result in all pods running in the 'hami' namespace without errors. You should be able to manage heterogeneous devices effectively through the HAMi interface.
codeSnippets:
helm repo add hami https://koordinator-sh.github.io/charts
helm repo update

kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: Role
metadata:
  namespace: hami
  name: hami-role
rules:
- apiGroups: ["*"]
  resources: ["pods", "pods/status", "pods/exec"]
  verbs: ["get", "list", "watch", "create", "delete", "update"]
EOF

kubectl create rolebinding hami-role-binding --role=hami-role --serviceaccount=hami:hami --namespace=hami

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a Kubernetes cluster running version 1.24 or higher, and have Helm and kubectl installed on your local machine.
